title: Pricing methods
summary: 9609 AS — cost-plus, penetration, skimming, competitive, and psychological pricing with cost floor links.
learningObjectives: Explain major pricing methods and when each applies.
Link pricing to costs and contribution (5.4).
Analyse price elasticity and competitive context.
Recommend pricing strategy for case scenarios.

simpleExplanation.title: What to charge
simpleExplanation.summary: Pricing methods balance costs, customer willingness to pay, competition, and strategic objectives — the wrong method can destroy margin or market share.
steps: Penetration: low launch price to gain share quickly.
Skimming: high launch price from early adopters.
Psychological: pricing cues that affect perception.

flashcards.back: Price = Unit cost + Mark-up (%). Simple but ignores demand and competition.
Low initial price to rapid market share; may sacrifice short-run profit.
High initial price; lowered over time as competition enters.
Set price relative to rivals — match, undercut, or premium.
£9.99 vs £10; prestige pricing; bundle pricing effects.
Long run: cover full cost; short run: cover variable cost/contribution minimum.
Innovative product, inelastic demand, few competitors, high R&D to recover.
Elastic demand, economies of scale, deter entrants, fast network effects.

sections.content: **Pricing methods** (3.3.4) bridges **marketing** and **finance (5.4)**. Examiners reward answers that cite **contribution/break-even** alongside **marketing rationale**.

items: **Cost-plus** — ensures margin; ignores demand (common in B2B/contracts).
**Penetration** — low price, high volume; needs scale.
**Skimming** — high price first; technology and fashion examples.

sections.question:
Variable cost $12 per unit; full unit cost $18; competitors charge $22–$24. A new entrant wants rapid market share in a price-sensitive market.

Recommend a pricing method and note the cost constraint.
sections.solution:
**Recommend penetration pricing** — launch near **$20** (undercut rivals) to build volume quickly in **elastic** market.

**Cost constraint:** Price must exceed **variable cost ($12)** in short run for **positive contribution**. At $20, contribution = $8 per unit toward fixed costs (**5.4.4 break-even**).

**Risk:** If price stays below **full cost ($18)** long term, business unsustainable unless scale cuts unit cost. Plan to **raise price** or **cut costs** once share secured.

sections.content: In **recommend** questions: name method → link to **case objective** → state **cost floor** → note **one risk**.
